title: [Security]: /metrics endpoint default-unauthenticated exposes multi-tenant PII in production deployments
body:
## Summary

The `/metrics` Prometheus endpoint is **unauthenticated by default** and exposes sensitive multi-tenant data in production LiteLLM Proxy deployments. While an opt-in authentication mechanism exists (`require_auth_for_metrics_endpoint: true`), the insecure default leads to widespread real-world exposure.

This is a follow-up to #13644, which was auto-closed by the stale bot as `NOT_PLANNED` without any maintainer response — despite multiple users confirming the security impact.

## Vulnerability Details

**Type:** Information Disclosure / Tenant Isolation Bypass  
**Default behavior:** `/metrics` endpoint serves ~2MB of Prometheus metrics to any unauthenticated HTTP request  
**CVSS 3.1:** 7.5 (High) — `AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:N/A:N`

## Data Exposed via `litellm_proxy_total_requests_metric` Labels

The Prometheus metrics include per-request labels that leak the following data for **every tenant**:

| Label | Sensitive Data |
|-------|---------------|
| `hashed_api_key` | SHA-256 hash of customer API keys |
| `api_key_alias` | Human-readable key names (often containing company names) |
| `team` | Team UUIDs |
| `team_alias` | Company names and **employee email addresses** (PII) |
| `end_user` | End-user identifiers |
| `user` | User identifiers with email addresses |
| `requester_ip_address` | Client IP addresses revealing infrastructure topology |
| `user_agent` | Full user-agent strings (exposing Azure Logic Apps workflow IDs, automation tools) |
| `model` | AI models accessed per customer |
| `model_id` | Internal model UUIDs |

## Real-World Impact (Verified)

During authorized security testing, we identified production LiteLLM Proxy deployments where the unauthenticated `/metrics` endpoint exposed:

- **15+ customer tenants** with complete operational telemetry
- **Corporate email addresses** in `team_alias` fields (GDPR-relevant PII)
- **Client IP addresses** enabling infrastructure geolocation and cloud provider identification
- **Azure Logic Apps workflow IDs** in user-agent strings, enabling reverse engineering of customer automation infrastructure
- **API key hashes** with aliases mapping to specific companies
- **Cross-tenant request patterns** enabling competitive intelligence gathering

A single unauthenticated GET request returns the complete dataset for all tenants.

## Root Cause

The `/metrics` endpoint is registered without any authentication middleware by default. The existing `require_auth_for_metrics_endpoint` setting is:

1. **Opt-in** rather than opt-out (insecure by default)
2. **Not prominently documented** in deployment guides
3. **Not enabled in the default/example configurations**

This violates the principle of **secure by default** — especially critical for a multi-tenant proxy handling API credentials.

## Recommendation

1. **Make authentication the default** for `/metrics` — require explicit opt-out (`allow_unauthenticated_metrics: true`) rather than opt-in authentication
2. **Sanitize metric labe

title: [Minter] Add solo staking
body:
Users should be able to use our contract for solo staking. 
To start we would require users to have the following: 
- 32 ETH
- 1000 SGT bonded and timelocked in a way that admins can unlock it when the users exit their validators or slash it if the user acts in a nefarious way.   

For this we will need to upgrade the send2eth2 function in the minter to use AccessControl over having a single owner.  

For a simplified implementation, the SGT bonded user should be able to call the function directly, resulting in a deposit function call which returns 32 vETH2 to them in exchange for 32.1 ETH.  

The solo staker can then use their vETH2 to earn profits at a higher APY in our ecosystem while waiting for ETH2 rewards to be unlocked via a chain merge or other mechanism the EF comes up with.  

This way the solo staker holds onto both their private keys for withdrawals and the validator. 

The solo staker will be responsible for running their own validator to start with and entering the correct public keys for the call registering the validator.

title: Bounty: Implement InkCanvas and InkPresenter controls (lifetime commercial license reward)
body:
## Bounty
Whoever fully implements `InkCanvas` + `InkPresenter` parity for this repository will receive a **lifetime commercial license** for InkkSlinger.

## Why this issue is combined
`InkPresenter` is the stroke-rendering primitive hosted by `InkCanvas`. In WPF terms they are tightly coupled. In this repository, implementing them together in one PR avoids partial API surfaces that are hard to validate.

## Target outcome
Deliver a practical MVP parity slice where users can:
- place an `InkCanvas` in XML/XAML,
- draw strokes with pointer input,
- see strokes rendered and clipped correctly,
- keep behavior deterministic with focused regression tests,
- keep full solution + test suite green.

## Repository orientation (start here)
Read these files first:
- `README.md`
- `TODO.md`
- `UI-FOLDER-MAP.md`

### High-value local references for implementation patterns
- Input routing and focus/capture orchestration:
  - `UI/Managers/Root/Services/UiRootInputPipeline.cs`
  - `UI/Input/Core/FocusManager.cs`
- Base control and rendering lifecycle:
  - `UI/Controls/Base/UIElement.cs`
  - `UI/Controls/Base/Control.cs`
  - `UI/Controls/Base/FrameworkElement.cs`
- Pointer-handling control examples:
  - `UI/Controls/Inputs/RichTextBox.cs`
  - `UI/Controls/Scrolling/ScrollViewer.cs`
  - `UI/Controls/Inputs/Slider.cs`
  - `UI/Controls/Containers/DocumentViewer.cs`
- Rendering helpers:
  - `UI/Rendering/Core/UiDrawing.cs`
  - `UI/Controls/Primitives/Shape.cs`
- XAML object creation and property-element plumbing:
  - `UI/Xaml/Core/XamlLoader.cs`
- Schema authoring:
  - `Schemas/InkkSlinger.UI.xsd`
- Recently added reference pattern for introducing a new control end-to-end:
  - `UI/Controls/Containers/DocumentViewer.cs`
  - `InkkSlinger.Tests/DocumentViewer*Tests.cs`

## Suggested implementation architecture
This is a recommended shape, not a strict requirement.

### 1) Core ink model types (new)
Add small, testable model types under `UI/Text/Viewing/` or `UI/Input/` or another coherent location (maintainer discretion):
- `InkStroke`
  - `IReadOnlyList<Vector2>` points
  - stroke color/thickness/opacity
  - optional bounding box cache
- `InkStrokeCollection`
  - add/remove/clear
  - change notifications for invalidation hooks
- optional `InkDrawingAttributes` style object if you want WPF-like configuration

Keep allocations controlled on pointer-move hot paths.

### 2) `InkPresenter` control (new)
`InkPresenter` should be the rendering primitive for stroke collections:
- derive from `Control`
- expose DP(s) for stroke source / stroke visual settings
- render strokes in `OnRender(SpriteBatch)`
- support clipping (`TryGetClipRect`) consistent with surrounding controls
- invalidate minimally when stroke collection mutates

### 3) `InkCanvas` control (new)
`InkCanvas` should host `InkPresenter` and own interaction semantics:
- derive from `Control` (or container base if required)
- expose DP(s) for stroke collection and editing mode
- pointer interaction path:
  - pointe

title: Title: Invalid threshold input (“$ of X”) triggers fallback mode that duplicates entropy and leaks checksum-index hiding mechanism (significant implementation bug)
body:
Hi Bitaps team,
I’m a self-taught BTC learner who has been testing your Mnemonic Secret Sharing tool at [bitaps.com](http://bitaps.com/) (the one linked from the 1 BTC Shamir challenge page). While playing with the “Mnemonic secret sharing schema threshold” form I discovered unexpected behavior that appears to be a significant implementation bug.
Steps to reproduce:
1.  Go to the Mnemonic secret sharing tool.
2.  In the input box, paste any 12-word mnemonic (I tested both my own phrases and the two public challenge shares concatenated as one 24-word string).
3.  Set the threshold to something invalid like $ of 5 or $ of 8 (or any non-numeric value in the first box).
4.  Click “Calculate”.
5.  Repeat “Calculate” several times.
Expected behavior:
The tool should either reject the invalid threshold with a clear error (“threshold must be a number between 1 and 255”) or refuse to generate shares, since proper Shamir requires a valid integer threshold.
Actual behavior (what I observed every time):
•  The tool silently generates the exact number of “shares” I typed in the “of X” box (5, 8, etc.).
•  Every share is identical in the first 11 words (the entropy part is simply duplicated).
•  Only the 12th word (checksum) changes.
•  Repeatedly hitting Calculate makes the 12th word cycle alphabetically through valid BIP39 words (example cycle I captured on multiple runs): funny → fury → furnace → future → gadget → gain → galaxy → gap → garlic → garment → game → garden → garage → garbage → etc.
•  Changing the last word of my input phrase (e.g. to something starting with “d”) shifts the entire cycle to words starting with that letter.
•  The cycle length seems capped around 16 possibilities, which matches the 4 checksum bits described in your BIP/mnemonic-improvement.md.
This happens with any input, including the two public challenge shares you published.
Why this is a bug (and why it matters for the challenge): Your own documentation explains that share indices are hidden in the 4 checksum bits of the last word so every share still looks like a normal BIP39 mnemonic. In normal operation (valid 3-of-5 threshold) the tool should run real GF(256) Shamir polynomials and produce completely different entropy in each share.
When the threshold is invalid, it falls back to this trivial “duplicate + cycle checksum for index” mode. This exposes the index-hiding trick in plain view and shows that input validation is missing (NaN checks are not handled, so the code skips the real sharing logic).
This matches the “significant implementation bug” category in your bug bounty (0.05 BTC+ tier) and could potentially weaken share indistinguishability or allow reconstruction tricks from fewer than the required shares.
Impact:
•  Makes the tool behave unpredictably on bad input.
•  Reveals internal mechanics that should stay hidden.
•  Possibly related to why no one has claimed the main 1 BTC challenge yet (the fallback could be exploitable).
Suggested fix:
•  Add proper type-c

title: Implement Lifting Wavelet Transform (LWT) and Inverse LWT (ILWT)
body:
# Implement Lifting Wavelet Transform (LWT) and Inverse LWT (ILWT)

Implement an optimized LWT and ILWT kernels for TT-Metal/TTNN on Wormhole/Blackhole hardware, which would enable usage of discrete wavelet transforms (DWTs) on device.

## 📝 Background

Discrete wavelet transform will help us enable multi-resolution signal processing directly on Wormhole/Blackhole hardware. This unlocks:
- JPEG2000 lossy compression.
- Real-time Digital Signal Processing pipelines.
- Audio, biosignals, radar, and seismic data processing applications.

## 🎯 What Success Looks Like

Requirements
- Support 1D/2D inputs with batch dimension (B, 1, H, W).
  - Layout can be both row major and tile. Preferably RM for 1d input, and tile layout for 2d input.
- Support the wavelets from PyWavelets catalog:
  - Haar: db1
  - Daubechies: dbN
  - Biorthogonal (used in JPEG2000): biorN.M
  - Symlets: symN
  - Coiflets: coifN
  - Discrete Meyer: dmey
- Support fp32 (SFPU-based implementation)
- Should be implemented as ttnn operation (`ttnn.dwt`/`ttnn.idwt`)
  - Should be implemented as optimized multicore kernel (not a naive composite version)
- Precision targets for the SFPU fp32 (Wavelet categories are defined in [the table below](#wavelet-categories)):
- Compact lifting schemes:
  - Definition: `tap_size <= 12`, `num_steps <= 11`, and `max_abs_coeff < 1e2`.
  - Forward transformation precision should be measured against PyWavelets using L2 relative error:
    - target median L2 relative error < 1e-7;
    - target 95th percentile L2 relative error < 1e-5.
  - Reconstruction precision should be measured against the original signal using L2 relative error:
    - target median L2 relative error < 1e-7;
    - target 95th percentile L2 relative error < 1e-5.

- Medium lifting schemes:
  - Definition: `12 < tap_size <= 24`, `12 <= num_steps <= 23`, and `max_abs_coeff < 1e4`.
  - Forward transformation precision should be measured against PyWavelets using L2 relative error:
    - target median L2 relative error < 1e-6;
    - target 95th percentile L2 relative error < 1e-4.
  - Reconstruction precision should be measured against the original signal using L2 relative error:
    - target median L2 relative error < 1e-6;
    - target 95th percentile L2 relative error < 1e-4.

- Large / numerically sensitive lifting schemes:
  - Definition: `tap_size > 24`, or `num_steps > 23`, or `max_abs_coeff >= 1e4`.
  - Forward precision is best effort.
  - Reconstruction precision is best effort.
  - These wavelets should report per-wavelet median / p95 / max L2 relative error and known outliers, but should not be treated as hard-gating failures.

- Precision reports should always include a per-wavelet breakdown and explicitly call out known SFPU fp32 outliers.


- No strict requirements for potential lower precision implementation.

- Optionally
  - Investigate FPU implementation (im2col/conv based) with tf32/bf16 + acc fp32 precision
  - Support l1 sharded inputs
  - Support arbitraty number of

title: [Feature Request]: Implement Lifting Wavelet Transform and Inverse LWT
body:
# Implement Lifting Wavelet Transform (LWT) and Inverse LWT (ILWT)

**By** @draklowell, @ke1rro, @kerfmanner, @sodaqqq *@ Ukrainian Catholic University*

## Summary
We target to implement an optimized LWT and ILWT kernels for TT-Metal to enable O(N) multi-resolution signal processing directly on Wormhole/Blackhole hardware. This unlocks efficient wavelet-based AI architectures (WaveKAN), JPEG2000 compression, and real-time Digital Signal Processing pipelines without CPU fallback. The implementation will be memory-aware and optimized for Tensix L1 constraints and NoC topology.

## Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe

While work on Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) is currently in progress within the tt-metal ecosystem ([#21412](https://github.com/tenstorrent/tt-metal/issues/21412)), FFT alone is insufficient for modern signal processing tasks dealing with **non-stationary signals**. FFT provides frequency information but completely loses temporal localization - it cannot tell _when_ a particular frequency event occurred.

The Discrete Wavelet Transform (DWT) applications are vast and critical for many domains:

- **Advanced Digital Signal Processing**: Processing audio, biosignals, radar, and seismic data where _when_ a frequency occurs is as important as _which_ frequency. Short-Time Fourier Transform (STFT) attempts to solve this but suffers from fixed resolution (Heisenberg uncertainty principle) and higher computational cost.
- **Next-Gen AI Models**: Emerging architectures like **WaveKAN** (Wavelet Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks) replace standard activation functions with wavelet bases to improve convergence, interpretability, and function approximation. Currently, there are no native ops to accelerate these models on Wormhole/Blackhole.
- **Signal Compression**: Wavelet transforms are the foundation of JPEG 2000 and modern audio codecs, offering superior compression without block artifacts compared to DCT-based methods.
- **Denoising**: Wavelet thresholding is one of the most effective techniques for noise removal across domains - from medical imaging to financial time series.

## Describe the solution you'd like

We propose implementing Discrete Wavelet Transform via **LWT** and **ILWT** as a logical extension to the DSP capabilities being built around FFT.

Our team has been exploring the **Lifting Scheme** (Sweldens, 1996) as the implementation approach.

The Lifting Scheme decomposes the transform into three simple steps that are done in-place:
- Split: Separate even/odd indexed samples.
- Predict: Predict odd samples from even (generates detail coefficients).
- Update: Update even samples (generates approximation coefficients).

![Lifting scheme (from Wikipedia)](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/b011cc22-0709-4bb9-a8df-8586bc583ab4)

### Why Lifting Scheme?

We believe this is the right fit for Tenstorrent hardware for the following reasons:
| Property | Fast Wavelet Transform | Lifting Wavelet Transform |
|--

title: [Security #5] Unsafe Deserialization in Mint Cache (High)
body:
---
title: "Security #5: Unsafe Deserialization in Mint Cache (High)"
labels: ["security", "bug", "mint", "cache", "deserialization"]
assignees: []
---

## 🚨 Security Vulnerability Report #5

**Severity:** High  
**Component:** Mint Cache Layer  
**File:** `cashu/mint/cache.py` (line 54)  
**Impact:** Potential RCE, data manipulation, cache poisoning  
**Bounty:** Requesting payment in Cashu ecash  

**Related:** Issue #921, #922, #924, #925 (same researcher - ongoing audit)

---

## Summary

The mint cache layer uses `json.loads()` on data retrieved from Redis without proper validation. If an attacker can inject malicious data into the Redis cache, they could manipulate mint operations or potentially execute arbitrary code.

---

## Technical Details

### Vulnerable Code

**File:** `cashu/mint/cache.py`  
**Line:** 54

```python
async def wrapper(*args, **kwargs):
    # ...
    resp = await self.redis.get(key)
    if resp:
        return json.loads(resp)  # ← VULNERABLE: Unsafe deserialization
    else:
        raise Exception(f"Found no cached response for key {key}")
```

### The Bug

Data retrieved from Redis is directly deserialized with `json.loads()` without:
1. Validating the data structure
2. Checking data integrity
3. Verifying the source

### Attack Vector

1. Attacker gains ability to write to Redis (direct access, SSRF, etc.)
2. Injects malicious serialized data
3. Mint reads and deserializes the data
4. Attacker controls mint behavior

---

## Impact

**Severity:** High

**Potential Impact:**
- Mint cache poisoning
- Manipulation of mint operations
- Potential for RCE if combined with other vulnerabilities
- Loss of funds if quote/proof data is manipulated

**Affected Users:**
- All mint users
- Wallet holders

---

## Recommended Fix

### Validate Data Structure Before Deserialization

```python
async def wrapper(*args, **kwargs):
    resp = await self.redis.get(key)
    if resp:
        # Validate data structure before deserializing
        try:
            data = json.loads(resp)
            # Add type/structure validation here
            if not isinstance(data, dict):
                raise ValueError("Invalid cache data type")
            return data
        except json.JSONDecodeError:
            logger.error(f"Invalid JSON in cache for key {key}")
            raise
    else:
        raise Exception(f"Found no cached response for key {key}")
```

---

## Affected Versions

- Nutshell v0.20.0 (confirmed)
- Earlier versions (likely affected)

---

## Bounty Information

**Requested Payout:** Cashu ecash or Lightning Bitcoin  
**Estimated Value:** $1,000-$3,000 (High severity)  

**My Lightning Address:**  
```
npub1sh9jm4ztw9a9g4wnqfacd4749r54agxastujhs6t2r0lgel0a7esjkd4fz@npub.cash
```

---

## Disclosure Timeline

- **2026-03-24:** Vulnerability discovered by automated scanner
- **2026-03-25:** Reported to Cashu team (Issue #926)
- **2026-04-XX:** Planned public disclosure (90 days)

---

### Executive Summary
Gitcoin Grants pioneered the use of Quadratic Funding (QF) at scale, turning a cryptographic trick into a sustainable public goods funding engine. Over 300 rounds, it distributed more than $50M to thousands of open source projects, from Ethereum core clients to frontend libraries. This case study examines the model’s mechanics, its impact on the Ethereum ecosystem, and key lessons for future funding systems.

### The Problem
Open source maintainers face chronic underfunding. Donations are sporadic, grants are cumbersome, and corporate sponsorship is narrow. Gitcoin set out to prove that a decentralized matching pool could fairly amplify small contributions, aligning incentives across builders, users, and stakeholders.

### The Quadratic Funding Solution
Instead of a simple 1:1 match, Gitcoin Grants used the formula:

> **Matching for project i** = (∑√cᵢ)²

Where cᵢ are each individual’s contribution. This mathematically ensures:
- Broad support beats large whales
- The influence of a $100 contribution is equivalent to ten $10 contributions
- The matching pool is allocated efficiently (in theory, it maximizes plurality utility)

## 🚨 Security Vulnerability Report #6

**Severity:** High  
**Component:** Core Base Layer

**Impact:** Data manipulation, potential code execution  
**Bounty:** Requesting payment in Cashu ecash  

**Related:** Issue #921, #922, #924, #925, #926 (same researcher)

---

## Summary

The core base layer uses `json.loads()` on database row data without proper validation, potentially allowing attackers to manipulate critical financial data.

---

## Technical Details

### Vulnerable Code

**File:** `cashu/core/base.py`  
**Line:** 324

```python
outputs = None
if "outputs" in row.keys() and row["outputs"]:
    outputs = json.loads(row["outputs"])  # ← VULNERABLE

return cls(
    quote=row["quote"],
    method=row["method"],
    request=row["request"],
```

### The Bug

Database-stored JSON is deserialized without validation. If an attacker can manipulate database contents, they could:
1. Inject malicious serialized data
2. Manipulate transaction outputs
3. Potentially exploit deserialization vulnerabilities

---

## Impact

**Severity:** High

**Potential Impact:**
- Transaction output manipulation
- Financial data tampering
- Potential for code execution (depending on JSON content)

**Affected Users:**
- All wallet users
- Mint operators

---

## Recommended Fix

```python
outputs = None
if "outputs" in row.keys() and row["outputs"]:
    try:
        outputs_data = json.loads(row["outputs"])
        # Validate structure
        if not isinstance(outputs_data, list):
            raise ValueError("outputs must be a list")
        # Validate each output
        for output in outputs_data:
            if not isinstance(output, dict) or "amount" not in output:
                raise ValueError("Invalid output format")
        outputs = outputs_data
    except json.JSONDecodeError as e:
        logger.error(f"Invalid JSON in outputs: {e}")
```

Hi **@faizzyhon**,

First off, thank you for developing **BUGSCANNER**. It’s a powerful tool for **PHP web security** and **bug bounty hunting**.

To make the tool even more accessible and versatile, I would like to suggest the implementation of a GUI mode. While the CLI is efficient for automation, a visual interface would provide several benefits:

- Ease of Use: Allows users who are less comfortable with the terminal to leverage the scanner’s power.

- Real-time Visualization: A dashboard to view scan progress, categorized vulnerabilities (Critical, High, Medium, Low), and live logs.

- Configuration Management: A visual way to toggle specific scan modules or input target lists without managing complex flags.

Implementation Ideas:

- A lightweight desktop framework like PyQt or Tkinter (if moving toward a Python-based wrapper).

- Alternatively, a Web-based UI (using a local Flask or FastAPI server) that displays the results in a browser.

I believe this addition would significantly increase the project's adoption within the security community. I’d love to hear your thoughts on this or if you'd be open to community contributions specifically for a UI layer.

Keep up the great work!

title: [Security #8] Early Return in Mint Tasks Skips Validation (Medium-High)
body:
---
title: "Security #8: Early Return in Mint Tasks Skips Validation (Medium-High)"
labels: ["security", "bug", "mint", "validation", "tasks"]
assignees: []
---

## 🚨 Security Vulnerability Report #8

**Severity:** Medium-High  
**Component:** Mint Background Tasks  
**File:** `cashu/mint/tasks.py` (line 53)  
**Impact:** Payment processing bypass, state inconsistency  
**Bounty:** Requesting payment in Cashu ecash  

**Related:** Issue #921, #922, #924, #925, #926, #927 (same researcher)

---

## Summary

The mint's background task for processing invoice callbacks returns early when a quote is not found, without proper validation or state management.

---

## Technical Details

### Vulnerable Code

**File:** `cashu/mint/tasks.py`  
**Line:** 53

```python
async def _process_invoice_callback(
    checking_id: str,
    conn: Connection | None = None,
):
    # ...
    quote = await get_quote_byCheckId(
        checking_id=checking_id, db=self.db, conn=conn
    )
    if not quote:
        logger.error(f"Quote not found for {checking_id}")
        return  # ← VULNERABLE: Early return without proper handling
```

### The Bug

When a quote is not found:
1. Function logs an error
2. **Returns immediately**
3. **No state update**
4. **No cleanup**
5. **No notification**

This could lead to inconsistent mint state and potential race conditions.

---

## Impact

**Severity:** Medium-High

**Potential Impact:**
- Payment processing bypass
- Mint state inconsistency
- Race condition exploitation
- Potential double-spend

**Affected Users:**
- Mint operators
- Wallet users

---

## Recommended Fix

```python
if not quote:
    logger.error(f"Quote not found for {checking_id}")
    # Update state to reflect error
    await mark_invoice_as_failed(checking_id)
    # Notify administrators
    await notify_admin(f"Invalid checking_id: {checking_id}")
    return
```

---

**Requested Payout:** Cashu ecash or Lightning Bitcoin  
**Estimated Value:** $500-$2,000 (Medium-High severity)

title: [SECURITY DISCLOSURE] Critical Vulnerability found in ERC6909.sol
body:
**Enterprise-Grade Bug Report**

**Contract:** ERC6909
**Version:** 0.8.0
**Audited On:** 21 March 2026

**Introduction:**
This audit was conducted on the provided Solmate-based ERC6909 contract implementation. The audit focused on identifying potential critical and high-severity security vulnerabilities.

**Findings:**

### Critical Severity

1. **Reentrancy Vulnerability** in transferFrom function:
The `transferFrom` function uses a conditional statement to check if the sender has sufficient allowance to transfer the specified amount. However, this check is performed before updating the `balanceOf` and `allowance` mappings. This allows a malicious user to repeatedly call `transferFrom` in a loop, each time reducing the sender's allowance, ultimately causing a reentrancy attack.

**Mitigation:**
To mitigate this vulnerability, reentrancy-safe operations should be performed within a lock or using the OpenZeppelin reentrancy modifier.

2. **Arithmetic Overflow Vulnerability** in balanceOf and allowance mappings:
The contract uses mappings to store balance and allowance data. However, the data type used for this mapping (uint256) may cause arithmetic overflows when the balances or allowance amounts approach the maximum limit of uint256.

**Mitigation:**
To mitigate this vulnerability, consider using data types with a larger maximum value or using libraries that handle arithmetic overflows safely, such as SafeMath or OpenZeppelin's SafeMath library.

### High Severity

1. **Missing Input Validation** in transfer and transferFrom functions:
The `transfer` and `transferFrom` functions lack proper input validation. Specifically, they do not check if the `receiver` address is not the zero address (0x0).

**Mitigation:**
To mitigate this vulnerability, add input validation to check for zero addresses and other invalid conditions.

2. **Incorrect Gas Optimizations in setOperator function:**
The `setOperator` function uses a mapping to store operator status, which can lead to excessive storage growth and associated gas costs over time.

**Mitigation:**
To mitigate this vulnerability, consider using a Merkle tree or other gas-efficient data structures to store operator status.

3. **Missing Authorization Checks** in certain functions:
Functions like `approve` and `transfer` lack proper authorization checks.

**Mitigation:**
To mitigate this vulnerability, add authorization checks to ensure that only authorized parties can execute these functions.

**Recommendations:**

To address these vulnerabilities, consider the following recommendations:

1. Apply reentrancy-safe operations within a lock or using the OpenZeppelin reentrancy modifier.
2. Use data types with a larger maximum value or libraries that handle arithmetic overflows safely.
3. Implement input validation for zero addresses and other invalid conditions in all functions.

title: [SECURITY DISCLOSURE] Critical Vulnerability found in ERC6909.sol
body:
**BUG REPORT**

**Contract Name:** ERC6909
**Severity:** HIGH
**Report Date:** March 21, 2026

**Summary:**
The ERC6909 contract is vulnerable to several high-severity bugs, including unguarded transfers, approval bypassing, and operator mismanagement. These vulnerabilities can be exploited by malicious users to drain assets, steal approvals, and manipulate operator status.

**Vulnerabilities:**

1. **Unprotected Transfer**
	* **Severity:** HIGH
	* **Description:** The `transfer` function does not check if the sender has sufficient balance before performing the transfer. This allows an attacker to transfer more assets than they own, potentially draining the contract's assets.
	* **Exploit:** An attacker can call `transfer` with a malicious `amount` parameter, exceeding their own balance.
	* **Recommendation:** Add a balance check before performing the transfer: `require(balanceOf[msg.sender][id] >= amount, "Insufficient balance");`
2. **Approval Bypass**
	* **Severity:** HIGH
	* **Description:** The `transferFrom` function does not properly check the allowance when the sender is not the same as the message sender. This allows an attacker to bypass the approval mechanism and steal assets.
	* **Exploit:** An attacker can call `transferFrom` with a malicious `sender` parameter, bypassing the approval check.
	* **Recommendation:** Update the allowance check to account for the sender: `if (msg.sender != sender && !isOperator[sender][msg.sender]) { ... }` should be `if (msg.sender != sender && (allowance[sender][msg.sender][id] < amount || !isOperator[sender][msg.sender])) { ... }`
3. **Operator Mismanagement**
	* **Severity:** HIGH
	* **Description:** The `setOperator` function allows an operator to be set without proper authorization. This can lead to unauthorized access to assets.
	* **Exploit:** An attacker can call `setOperator` to grant themselves operator status without the owner's consent.
	* **Recommendation:** Add a check to ensure only the owner can set operators: `require(msg.sender == owner, "Only the owner can set operators");`
4. **Reentrancy Vulnerability**
	* **Severity:** HIGH
	* **Description:** The `transfer` and `transferFrom` functions emit events, which can lead to reentrancy attacks. An attacker can exploit this to drain assets.
	* **Exploit:** An attacker can create a contract that listens for the `Transfer` event and reenters the `transfer` or `transferFrom` function.
	* **Recommendation:** Use a reentrancy lock or a more secure pattern, such as the Checks-Effects-Interactions pattern.
5. **Unsecured Internal Functions**
	* **Severity:** HIGH
	* **Description:** The `_mint` and `_burn` functions are not secured against external calls. This allows an attacker to manipulate asset balances.
	* **Exploit:** An attacker can call `_mint` or `_burn` directly to alter asset balances.

After a resume, when network gets disconnected, a logged in User can no longer unlock the lock screen. I even cannot switch consoles via ctrl + alt +f# to maybe fix it.

title: bug: ephemeral sessions break gemini-cli file access (wrong working directory)
body:
## Problem

When using `--ephemeral` with `--tool gemini-cli`, the spawned gemini-cli session cannot find any project files because the working directory is set to a temporary CSA session directory instead of the project root.

### Reproduction

```bash
cat <<'PROMPT' | csa run --sa-mode true --force-ignore-tier-setting --tool gemini-cli --idle-timeout 300 --ephemeral
Read crates/mandate-core/crates/mandate-bounty/fixtures/bounty.toml and report its contents.
PROMPT
```

### Observed Behavior

Gemini-cli spends all its time trying to locate files:

```
[stdout] I will list the directory contents of the project to confirm the file paths
[stdout] I will list the directory contents of the `mandate-monorepo` path
[stdout] I will search for the relevant files by name to find their exact location
[stdout] I will check the current working directory
[stdout] I will list the `/home/obj/project` directory
[stdout] I will use `find` to locate the `mandate-dev-drafts` directory
```

It never finds the files because `pwd` is a CSA temp directory, not the project root.

### Expected Behavior

Even in `--ephemeral` mode, the tool should be able to access project files. Options:
1. Pass `--cd <project-root>` to the tool
2. Set `cwd` in the tool invocation to the project root
3. Inject the project root path in the prompt context

### Environment

- CSA version: `csa 0.1.167 (v0.1.15-274-g1828e8b)`
- Tool: gemini-cli
- Flag: `--ephemeral`
- All 3 parallel ephemeral gemini-cli sessions exhibited the same behavior

### Impact

- `--ephemeral` mode is effectively unusable with gemini-cli for any task requiring file access
- Non-ephemeral sessions work correctly (gemini-cli can find files via the project's working directory)

### Workaround

Don't use `--ephemeral` for gemini-cli sessions that need file access.

title: Puzzle Crowdfund and Community Bounty Contract

**Location:** `contracts/puzzle_crowdfund`
**Estimated Time:** 7 hours
**Difficulty:** Hard

### Description
Allow the community to pool funds to create a shared bounty for solving a specific puzzle. Any player can contribute to a crowdfund campaign. When the target amount is reached, the bounty activates. The first player to solve the puzzle claims the full pooled reward. Unfunded campaigns can be refunded.

### Tasks
- [ ] Design `CrowdfundCampaign` struct: `puzzle_id`, `target_amount`, `raised`, `status`, `contributors: Map<Address, i128>`, `winner`
- [ ] Implement `create_campaign(puzzle_id, target_amount, deadline)` — initializes new campaign
- [ ] Implement `contribute(campaign_id, amount)` — adds to `raised`; stores contributor share
- [ ] Campaign auto-activates when `raised >= target_amount`
- [ ] Implement `claim_bounty(campaign_id, solution_hash)` — first valid solver takes all raised funds

## Findings

---

### DOC-01 — TECHNICAL.md describes a completely different contract API to what exists

**Severity:** Critical
**File:** `TECHNICAL.md`
**Lines:** 228–244

**Description:**
`TECHNICAL.md` documents the following Arena.sol functions in its key contract reference table:

| Function | Purpose |
|----------|---------|
| `registerAgent(address, bytes32 strategy)` | Register an agent |
| `recordScore(address, uint256)` | Update agent score |
| `claimRoundReward(uint256 roundId)` | Agents claim prize |
| `endRound(uint256 roundId)` | Settle scores |

None of these functions exist. The actual contract has `register(address[])`, `startRound()`, `executeBatch()`, and `settle()`. These are not naming differences — they represent fundamentally different designs. `claimRoundReward` implies agents pull their own prizes on-chain. `settle()` means the owner pushes prizes. The access model, the economic flow, and the agent interaction pattern differ entirely.

`TECHNICAL.md`'s Bounty.sol table (line 237–243) is equally misaligned: it documents `claimBounty(uint256 bountyId, bytes calldata proof)` (wrong signature — no proof param) and `rejectBounty(uint256 bountyId)` (this function does not exist anywhere in the codebase).

This document appears to describe an earlier design that was never built. Anyone building against `TECHNICAL.md` as a reference would implement against interfaces that do not exist.

**Recommended resolution:**
`TECHNICAL.md` should be fully rewritten to reflect the actual deployed contracts. Until then it should carry a prominent deprecation warning at the top.

---

### DOC-02 — Judge-facing documents make materially false claims about verification

**Severity:** Critical
**Files:** `TECHNICAL.md#L222`, `docs/JUDGES.md#L15`, `docs/JUDGES.md#L266`

**Description:**
Multiple judge-facing documents assert properties the contracts do not have.

`TECHNICAL.md` line 222:
> *"No off-chain verification. No human judges. The contract checks the pool's swaps log directly."*

`verifyAndPay()` is owner-only, accepts owner-supplied bytes, and never reads a pool or transaction log. Verification is entirely off-chain and owner-controlled.

title: [Idea Bounty] Nutrition Card: Prefill serving with last-used amount and compute nutrition facts based on weight
body:
## Feature Request
It's tedious to manually re-enter the serving/weight every time using the Nutrition Card. It should prefill the serving input with the last-used value (per food), and when the user edits the serving weight (e.g. grams), the nutrition facts line items should auto-compute corresponding macros for arbitrary fractional servings.

**Example:**
- If serving = 28g, and I enter 42g, the app should show protein, calories, etc. for 1.5 servings.
- If I log 100g cottage cheese today, tomorrow when I select it, the weight field should be prefilled with 100g (but still editable).
- Editing the amount should automatically recalc the nutrition facts displayed in the card instead of requiring a fixed unit.

---
- Use this issue as an end-to-end test for idea-credit + auto-queue, goal is fully automatic payout with NO maintainer approval required.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Nutrition Card's serving/weight field is prefilled with the last-used (per food, per user)
- [ ] Changing the weight/serving dynamically updates nutrition macros (calories, protein, etc.)
- [ ] If weight differs from 1.0 serving, all facts update proportionally
- [ ] Still allows manual override for edge cases

High-severity **economic attack** in TRON Stake 2.0. Floating point truncation bypasses `frozenV2BalanceForEnergy` validation, allowing **resource theft**.

actuator/src/main/java/org/tron/core/vm/nativecontract/DelegateResourceProcessor.java:79

long energyUsage = (long) (ownerCapsule.getEnergyUsage() * TRX_PRECISION *

title: Automated exploit detection with adaptive burn switch and researcher bounty
body:
## Summary
Implement automated exploit detection that triggers protocol burn mode and rewards security researchers who disclose vulnerabilities responsibly.

## Motivation
Recent exploit (`v17_commit_7817608_208` - async overlap manipulation) required manual intervention to activate burn mode. Miners are incentivized to probe the scanner; validators need automated defense that responds faster than human oversight.

## Proposed Mechanism

### 1. Automated Exploit Detection (Cron-based)
- **Frequency**: 15-minute intervals
- **Process**: 
  - Scan recent submissions for exploit patterns
  - Analyze AST for forbidden constructions (lazy loading, timer tampering, deferred computation)
  - Cross-reference against known exploit signatures
  - If confidence high → auto-trigger burn mode

### 2. Adaptive Burn Switch
- When exploit detected, validator automatically sets 100% burn rate
- Halts all emissions until patch deployed
- Logs detailed forensics (submission ID, exploit class, detection timestamp)

### 3. Researcher Bounty Program
- **Disclosure Reward**: TAO bounty for researchers who report exploits *responsibly* (before submitting to leaderboard)
- **Detection vs Exploitation**: 
  - Reporter shares proof-of-concept privately → receives bounty
  - Reporter exploits on mainnet → disqualified, no bounty
- **Bounty Amount**: TBD (suggested: 1-3 TAO for moderate severity, higher for critical)

## Implementation Notes
- Requires binding Ozymandias/discord analysis to validator governance layer
- Cron scanner must have authority to signal blockchain state changes
- Bounty distribution requires wallet integration or manual payout workflow

## Related
- Recent exploit report: [Discord thread](https://discord.com/channels/799672011265015819/1222226314824777853/1486147330507018310)
- Scanner rules: `src/crusades/core/security_defs.py`

---
*This issue formalizes the defense-in-depth strategy discussed with researchers who disclosed today's exploit.*

title: FIDO Login doesn't work, if user has cross device capable passkey too
body: ### Description Depending on the combination of authentication methods of a user, himmelblau doesn't allow a login with a Yubikey as passkey. On my account, I have the following authentication methods (via https://mysignins.microsoft.com/security-info): When logging in with Himmelblau, I am never given the option to authenticate with a Passkey, even though himmelblau would have the option for this. I am instead forwarded to the "Entra ID Password" screen. When I enter my password, I am shown a device code flow. As soon as I remove the last option (Passkey via Microsoft Authenticator), the login works fine and I can login via Yubikey. I believe that this is due to how `fido_is_a_passkey` is determined in libhimmelblau: as soon as for a user `has_cross_device_capable_passkey` is true, the FIDO logins are skipped. I attempted to fix this issue via https://gitlab.com/samba-team/libhimmelblau/-/merge_requests/144 and https://github.com/himmelblau-idm/himmelblau/pull/1220 ### Steps to Reproduce 1. Configure two different passkey methods on a user: a cross-device-capable one and a non-cross-device-capable one 2. Attempt to login via passkey -> fails 3. Remove the cross-device-capable one 4. title: Leads API does not return the owned contacts (uses createdBy instead of contact owner)
body:
### Mautic Series 6.0.x series ### Mautic installed version 6.0.8 ### Way of installing I downloaded a release from https://www.mautic.org/mautic-releases ### PHP version 8 ### What browsers are you seeing the problem on? _No response_ ### What happened? `GET /api/contacts` applies the wrong restriction for users who only have `lead:leads:viewown`. When a user does **not** have `lead:leads:viewother`, the API list endpoint limits results using the contact creator (`createdBy`) instead of the contact owner. A user can therefore fail to see contacts they own if those contacts were created by someone else. ### How can we reproduce this issue? ## Steps to reproduce 1. Create a user with `lead:leads:viewown` and **without** `lead:leads:viewother` permissions. 2. Create a contact with: - `owner` = that user - `createdBy` = a different user 3. Call `GET /api/contacts` authenticated as the restricted user. ## Expected behavior The owned contact appears in the list (same idea as “my contacts” / owner-based access). ## Actual behavior The contact is not returned. Granting `lead:leads:viewother` makes it appear, which confirms the list is being narrowed incorrectly for `viewown`. ## Why this is inconsistent - Entity-level checks use `Lead::getPermissionUser()` (`owner` first, then `createdBy` if no owner). - The contact UI treats “mine” as owner-based. - The API collection endpoint was filtering by `createdBy` only. ## Impact Integrations and API clients that rely on `/api/contacts` for owner-scoped access return incomplete or empty results for valid `viewown` users. ## Suggested direction Define “own” for contacts in the API list path using ownership (e.g. `owner_id`), or align it explicitly with `getPermissionUser()` (e.g. owner match, or unowned + created by current user), instead of `createdBy` alone. Aivie is working on this fix. ### Relevant log output ```shell ``` ### Code of Conduct - [x] I confirm that I have read and agree to follow this project's Code of Conduct
Care about this issue? Want to get it resolved sooner? If you are a member of Mautic, you can add some funds to the Bounties Project so that the person who completes this task can claim those funds once it is merged by a member of the core team! Read the docs here.

title: [Idea Bounty] Make DV% clickable to customize daily value targets + nutrients list
body:
## Overview

We want to make the Raw Intake card more personal and flexible:
- Users should be able to **click the DV% label** for any nutrient (protein, sugar, etc) to open a panel to customize their own daily value goal for that nutrient (e.g. adjust protein goal up, sugar max up/down, etc).
- Allow DV% table to be personalized:
  - Users can **remove nutrients** they don't want to track daily
  - And **add back in** tracks as desired (from a master list)

## Details & Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Clicking on any nutrient's DV% opens a dialog where the user can set a custom target or %
- [ ] Raw Intake card and table update calculations & displayed values based on the new custom goal
- [ ] User can hide/show nutrients from their daily intake table
- [ ] Customizations persist (localStorage OK)
- [ ] Defaults still match US FDA/WHO
- [ ] Advanced: allow import/export of custom goals for sharing between users (optional)

---
## Labels
bounty: 1 BNUT, idea-adopted, idea-credit: @TheJollyLaMa

---
This is a test of the automated Copilot + idea-credit workflow.

title: [Security] Critical vulnerability in backup encryption — requesting private disclosure channel
body:
Hi Flow Wallet team,

I'm a security researcher. During a review of the Flow Reference Wallet Chrome extension (currently distributed on Chrome Web Store), I identified multiple critical cryptographic vulnerabilities in the Google Drive backup encryption implementation.

These issues allow offline extraction and brute-force decryption of wallet private keys from cloud backups with minimal computational cost.

**Severity:** 2× Critical, 1× High
**Affected component:** Backup encryption (Google Drive cloud backup feature)
**Status:** Verified with working PoC against the production Chrome Web Store build (v2.9.4)

I do NOT want to disclose details publicly. The HackenProof bug bounty program for Flow Wallet is currently closed (83 days until reopening), and this repository has no SECURITY.md or private vulnerability reporting enabled.

Could you please provide a secure channel (email, private advisory, or similar) so I can share the full report and proof-of-concept?

Thank you.

@zzggo @lmcmz @caosbad

title: buffs/debuffs for stats and masteries [bounty: 60 XTR]
body:
Browsing units within dash view shouldn't have the unit stats and masteries in card B displayed with red or green colors, just with white, unless the unit is materialized/alive and those states/masteries have been buffed (green) or debuffed (red).
Currently, those stats and masteries don't seem to properly update to the real values when changing between units!

Also, hovering a stat or mastery should show a tooltip listing the things that changed it.
This should visually be tied in with the dark transparent background shown on hover.
This functionality has been before in the game but it eventually broke due to regression.

title: Goey Body upgrade revamp [bounty: 30 XTR]
body:
Gumble's upgraded passive, Goey Body, can be a little confusing, as it leaves a trap on death that *might* or *might not* affect allies, being it hard to check or remember at times.

The upgrade should allow Gumble to simply leap over units during moving phase (walking at least 2 hexagons).

title: [Enhancement]: Add SECURITY.md security policy
body:
## Enhancement

**Description:**
Add a `SECURITY.md` security policy to the repository. This provides a clear channel for responsible vulnerability disclosure and documents the project's security practices.

**Motivation:**
The project now has gosec and CodeQL scanning in CI. A security policy completes the picture by telling users and researchers how to report vulnerabilities and what to expect.

**Proposed Solution:**
Create `SECURITY.md` in the repository root covering:

1. **Supported Versions** — table showing which releases receive security patches (latest minor release only)
2. **Reporting a Vulnerability** — use GitHub Private Vulnerability Reporting (Settings > Security > Private vulnerability reporting). No email setup required.
3. **Response Timeline** — acknowledge within 48 hours, target patch within 30 days
4. **Security Measures** — document existing tooling: gosec scanning, CodeQL analysis, `go vet`, golangci-lint
5. **Scope** — define what constitutes a vulnerability for a CLI tool:
   - Command injection via crafted input
   - Token or credential leakage
   - Config file permission issues
   - Dependency vulnerabilities
   - Out of scope: issues requiring local access beyond normal CLI usage

**Scope:**
- **In scope:** Create SECURITY.md, enable GitHub Private Vulnerability Reporting if not already enabled
- **Out of scope:** Bug bounty programs, PGP key setup, third-party disclosure platforms

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- [ ] `SECURITY.md` exists in repository root
- [ ] Supported versions table is accurate
- [ ] Reporting channel uses GitHub Private Vulnerability Reporting
- [ ] Response timelines are documented
- [ ] Existing security tooling is listed
- [ ] Vulnerability scope is defined for a CLI tool context

title: Bug Bounty and Feature Request Program
body:
# Bug Bounty and Feature Request Program

> **Status:** Work in Progress - Not Confirmed Yet
> This document is under development and subject to change. Terms and conditions are not finalized.

## Program Overview

We value contributions from the community. This bounty program incentivizes developers to identify and resolve bugs, implement features, and improve our codebase.

## Eligibility

- Open to all contributors worldwide
- Employees and their direct family members are not eligible
- One submission per person per issue
- Contributors must follow the project's Code of Conduct

## Bounty Tiers

### Critical Severity
- **Reward:** $500 - $2,500
- **Criteria:** Security vulnerabilities, data loss, complete feature breakdown
- **Examples:** SQL injection, authentication bypass, data corruption

### High Severity
- **Reward:** $250 - $500
- **Criteria:** Significant functionality impairment, performance degradation
- **Examples:** Major bugs affecting core features, memory leaks

### Medium Severity
- **Reward:** $50 - $250
- **Criteria:** Partial feature failure, inconsistent behavior, usability issues
- **Examples:** Edge case bugs, UI inconsistencies, minor performance issues

### Low Severity
- **Reward:** $10 - $50
- **Criteria:** Minor bugs, documentation improvements, code quality enhancements
- **Examples:** Typos, broken links, code style improvements

### Feature Implementation
- **Reward:** Negotiable based on scope
- **Criteria:** New features aligned with project roadmap
- **Process:** Discuss scope in issue before starting work

## Submission Process

1. Check existing issues to avoid duplicates
2. Create a detailed issue with reproduction steps
3. Include environment details (OS, version, dependencies)
4. For security issues, report privately to security@example.com
5. Include a pull request with a proposed fix (optional but recommended)
6. Maintainers will review and assess bounty eligibility

## Payment Terms

- Payouts processed within 30 days of merge
- Minimum bounty: $10
- Payment via bank transfer, PayPal, or project-specific arrangement
- Taxes are the responsibility of the recipient

## Guidelines

- Do not publicly disclose vulnerabilities before they're fixed
- Provide clear, reproducible reports
- Be respectful in all communications
- One bounty per issue (no duplicate payments)
- Bounty amount determined at maintainers' discretion

## Important Notes

- This program is offered at our discretion and may be modified
- Bounties are non-negotiable after payout
- Quality of submission affects bounty amount
- Automated or low-effort submissions may be declined

---

For questions, contact: bounty@example.com

## Eligibility

- Open to all contributors worldwide
- Employees and their direct family members are not eligible
- One submission per person per issue
- Contributors must follow the project's Code of Conduct

## Bounty Tiers

### Critical Severity
- **Reward:** $500 - $2,500
- **Criteria:** Security vulnerabilities, data loss, complete feature breakdown
- **Examples:** SQL injection, authentication bypass, data corruption

### High Severity
- **Reward:** $250 - $500
- **Criteria:** Significant functionality impairment, performance degradation
- **Examples:** Major bugs affecting core features, memory leaks

### Medium Severity
- **Reward:** $50 - $250
- **Criteria:** Partial feature failure, inconsistent behavior, usability issues
- **Examples:** Edge case bugs, UI inconsistencies, minor performance issues

### Low Severity
- **Reward:** $10 - $50
- **Criteria:** Minor bugs, documentation improvements, code quality enhancements
- **Examples:** Typos, broken links, code style improvements

### Feature Implementation
- **Reward:** Negotiable based on scope
- **Criteria:** New features aligned with project roadmap
- **Process:** Discuss scope in issue before starting work

## Submission Process

1. Check existing issues to avoid duplicates
2. Create a detailed issue with reproduction steps
3. Include environment details (OS, version, dependencies)
4. For security issues, report privately to security@example.com
5. Include a pull request with a proposed fix (optional but recommended)
6. Maintainers will review and assess bounty eligibility

## Payment Terms

- Payouts processed within 30 days of merge
- Minimum bounty: $10
- Payment via bank transfer, PayPal, or project-specific arrangement
- Taxes are the responsibility of the recipient

## Guidelines

- Do not publicly disclose vulnerabilities before they're fixed
- Provide clear, reproducible reports
- Be respectful in all communications
- One bounty per issue (no duplicate payments)
- Bounty amount determined at maintainers' discretion

## Important Notes

- This program is offered at our discretion and may be modified
- Bounties are non-negotiable after payout
- Quality of submission affects bounty amount
- Automated or low-effort submissions may be declined

---

For questions, contact: bounty@example.com

title: Add SepsisPredictionEICU and SepsisPredictionMIMIC4 tasks — pre-ICU window, Sepsis-3 proxy labeling
body:
## Summary

PyHealth currently has no sepsis prediction task. This issue proposes adding two 
new task definitions:

- `SepsisPredictionEICU` — eICU Collaborative Research Database
- `SepsisPredictionMIMIC4` — MIMIC-IV

Both tasks target **pre-ICU / early ICU** detection windows, filling a confirmed 
gap identified in a PROSPERO-registered systematic review (CRD420251164609) covering 
14 studies (53,795 participants): nearly all existing sepsis ML models are trained 
on in-ICU data with no standardized reproducible benchmark.

## Clinical Motivation

Sepsis is defined by the Sepsis-3 consensus (Singer et al., JAMA 2016) as 
life-threatening organ dysfunction caused by dysregulated host response to infection. 
Early detection — before ICU admission or within the first hours — is where clinical 
impact is highest and where published ML models show the widest performance variance 
(AUROC 0.72–0.94 across studies), largely due to inconsistent cohort definitions 
and labeling strategies.

A reproducible PyHealth task enforces a consistent:
- **Observation window** (configurable: default 6h from first available data)
- **Prediction gap** (configurable: 0–12h before onset)
- **Sepsis label derivation** (Sepsis-3 proxy: ICD codes + SOFA ≥ 2 or 
  `apacheadmissiondx` for eICU)

## Proposed Implementation

### `SepsisPredictionEICU`
```python
input_schema = {
    "vitals": "time_series",     # HR, RR, MAP, SpO2, Temp from vitalPeriodic
    "labs": "sequence",          # WBC, lactate, creatinine from lab table
    "conditions": "sequence",    # apacheadmissiondx / pasthistory
    "demographics": "static",    # age, gender, admissionweight
}
output_schema = {
    "sepsis_label": "binary",
}
```

Label derivation from eICU:
- Positive: `apacheadmissiondx` containing sepsis/septic shock keywords **or** 
  ICD codes in `diagnosis` table (995.91, 995.92, A41.x)
- Negative: non-infectious admission diagnoses
- Excluded: ambiguous/missing diagnosis records

### `SepsisPredictionMIMIC4`
```python
input_schema = {
    "vitals": "time_series",     # chartevents: HR, RR, MAP, SpO2, GCS
    "labs": "sequence",          # labevents: WBC, creatinine, bilirubin, lactate
    "conditions": "sequence",    # diagnoses_icd (ICD-10)
    "demographics": "static",    # age, gender, admission_type
}
output_schema = {
    "sepsis_label": "binary",
}
```

Label derivation from MIMIC-IV:
- Use `sepsis3` flag from `mimiciv_derived.sepsis3` (already in MIMIC-IV derived 
  tables) where available
- Fallback: ICD-10 codes A40.x, A41.x in `diagnoses_icd`

## Deliverables

- [ ] `pyhealth/tasks/sepsis_prediction_eicu.py`
- [ ] `pyhealth/tasks/sepsis_prediction_mimic4.py`
- [ ] Export in `pyhealth/tasks/__init__.py`
- [ ] Unit tests in `tests/tasks/`
- [ ] Example notebook in `examples/sepsis_prediction/`
- [ ] Docstring with clinical references

## References

- Singer M et al. The Third International Consensus Definitions for Sepsis and 
  Septic Shock (Sepsis-3). JAMA. 2016;315(8):801–81

title: Incorrect Beneficiary Address in Child Bounty Payout, Funds Sent to Treasury-Derived Intermediary Account
body:
The error originated during the preparation of the second Child Bounty reimbursement transaction. While constructing the call, due to user oversight, the intermediary treasury-derived address from the previous successful transaction was reused instead of the intended DeFi Bounty address.

This mistake was not detected during multisig signing because:
- Due to the sunset and deprecation of existing multisig tooling (e.g., Mimir, Multix), the transaction was executed directly through Polkadot.js.
- The Polkadot.js UI truncates beneficiary addresses in the call preview.
- Only the first portion of the address was visible.
- The truncated address portion matched exactly between the intended beneficiary and the treasury-derived intermediary account `13UVJyLnbVp9RBZYFwH...`
- The differing part of the address was not immediately visible in the signing interface.

As a result, the multisig approved and executed the transaction with the incorrect beneficiary.

---

**Transaction Flow**

- The first payment for the Polkadot Hub ETH RPC service (OpsLayer, 1-year contract) was executed as Child Bounty 72 under Bounty 31:
[https://polkadot.subsquare.io/treasury/child-bounties/31_72?tab=timeline](https://polkadot.subsquare.io/treasury/child-bounties/31_72?tab=timeline)

The execution followed the standard Treasury flow:

1. Funds were released from the Bounty 31 account:
   [https://assethub-polkadot.subscan.io/account/13UVJyLnbVp9RBZYFwHYxa9vkLFAKkVW2R84Sq5poXZoCcN2](https://assethub-polkadot.subscan.io/account/13UVJyLnbVp9RBZYFwHYxa9vkLFAKkVW2R84Sq5poXZoCcN2)

2. The payout first transferred funds to a **treasury-derived intermediary account**:
   [https://assethub-polkadot.subscan.io/account/13UVJyLnbVp9RBZYFwHZ1tU5R4cAiBkUozfbaAvmb1YZvszc?tab=transfer](https://assethub-polkadot.subscan.io/account/13UVJyLnbVp9RBZYFwHZ1tU5R4cAiBkUozfbaAvmb1YZvszc?tab=transfer)

3. Within the same block, the funds were forwarded to the intended final beneficiary — the **DeFi Bounty account**:
   [https://assethub-polkadot.subscan.io/account/13UVJyLnbVp9RBZYFwHYxaAqqaywnygJs1K8H73FXaR6esrd](https://assethub-polkadot.subscan.io/account/13UVJyLnbVp9RBZYFwHYxaAqqaywnygJs1K8H73FXaR6esrd)

This confirms that the two-step Treasury mechanism (Bounty → Treasury intermediary → Final beneficiary) functioned correctly in the first transaction.

---

**The Error**

In the second CB, due to user error, the final beneficiary address was incorrectly set.

Instead of specifying the DeFi Bounty account, the **previous intermediary treasury address** was mistakenly used as the final beneficiary.

As a result:

* Funds were transferred from Bounty 31
* They were sent to the same **treasury-derived intermediary account**
* The funds are now sitting in the intermediary account.

The erroneous extrinsic can be seen here:
[https://assethub-polkadot.subscan.io/extrinsic/12676181-2](https://assethub-polkadot.subscan.io/extrinsic/12676181-2)



---

title: [Bug] Payroll panel: 'This issue has already been paid on-chain.' error blocks batch settlement
body:
## Problem

In the Payroll panel, we're seeing the following blocking error when attempting to settle a batch with open bounties:

```
⚠️ This issue has already been paid on-chain.
```

This prevents completion of the payout batch until we investigate and resolve the data issue. Likely cause: legacy or messy BNUT data in `payroll-queue.json` or `contributor-accounts.json` from past experiments with the idea-credit split bot logic (duplicate or stale entries; already paid batch hashes; etc).

## Steps to Reproduce
1. Go to the Payroll admin panel
2. Try to settle the next open payout batch
3. Error appears and settlement is blocked

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Investigate why the payout batch contains issues marked as already paid on-chain
- [ ] Correct data hygiene for payout queue and contributor accounts files
- [ ] Ensure no duplicate batch hashes/paid issues block future settlement
- [ ] Document the fix so similar data problems are easy to debug in future

---
**This is a critical blocker for payroll ops!**

title: OCI Metadata Labels Broken When Using Hyperlinks
body:
### Describe the bug Hey @baronfel, Please refer to [this discussion](https://github.com/dotnet/sdk/discussions/52087): when using the .NET SDK Container Building Tools and using hyperlinks, this breaks the OCI metadata labels. For example, please see this image manifest of a container image built using the .NET SDK Container Building Tools: ```json { // remainder left out for brevity "labels": { "org.opencontainers.image.source": "https", "com.docker.extension.changelog": "https", "org.opencontainers.image.url": "https", "org.opencontainers.image.licenses": "https", "org.opencontainers.image.documentation": "https", "com.docker.extension.publisher-url": "https", } } ``` But I properly ensured via some MSBuild output on GitHub Actions that the necessary properties are present: ``` GITHUB_REPOSITORY_OWNER: 'mu88' GITHUB_SERVER_URL: 'https://github.com' GITHUB_REPOSITORY: 'mu88/RaspiFanController' GITHUB_SHA: '0780e22d89107421e164448d2f249d8a8c5e27ff' ContainerAuthors: 'mu88' ContainerDocumentationUrl: 'https://github.com/mu88/RaspiFanController/blob/0780e22d89107421e164448d2f249d8a8c5e27ff/README.md' ContainerInformationUrl: 'https://github.com/mu88/pkgs/container/RaspiFanController' ContainerLicenseExpression: 'https://github.com/mu88/blob/0780e22d89107421e164448d2f249d8a8c5e27ff/LICENSE.md' ContainerRepository: 'mu88/RaspiFanController' ContainerTitle: 'mu88/RaspiFanController' ContainerVendor: 'mu88' ContainerVersion: '0780e22d89107421e164448d2f249d8a8c5e27ff' ``` ### To Reproduce 1. Create a new .NET project. 2. Publish a **multi-arch** Docker image via `dotnet publish /t:PublishContainer /p:RuntimeIdentifiers=linux-x64;linux-arm64 /p:ContainerDocumentationUrl=https://dot.net`. 3. title: WebLLM Genie Chat: Contextual AI Assistant Embedded in Side Panels (v3)
body:
## Overview
Build a new feature for v3.0.0 that places a small genie icon in the inside upper corner of each side panel. Clicking the genie expands an attached chat window powered by WebLLM, providing contextual assistance using the user's BigNuten data (e.g., logged exercises, diet, supplement lists, past workouts).

## Details
- **Genie Icon:**
  - Shown in inside upper corner of side panels when open if AI chat is enabled.
  - Clicking expands a chat window attached to the edge of the current side panel.
  - Genie icon and chat visually distinct from main panel UI; should not overlap main content.

- **WebLLM Chat Integration:**
  - Runs a quantized LLM clientside (WebLLM).
  - On first enable, downloads the model and stores it for future fast startup.
  - Chat window uses context from user's own BigNuten data:
    - Exercise history (esp. last 3 months)
    - Diet/supplement logs
    - Any custom or recommended values
  - Example usages:
    - "What should my daily values be given my logged exercises?"
    - "Any recommendations for my diet or supplements list?"
    - "Be my workout assistant—what have I not worked lately?"

- **UI/UX:**
  - Add a toggle switch to the Settings tab (upper right in header) to enable/disable the AI assistant. When off, genie icon/chat does not show and the model is not loaded.
  - Genie/chat UI clean, approachable, and fits BigNuten's look/feel.
  - Open/close state of chat persists per side panel.

- **Technical Notes:**
  - Respect all BigNuten privacy best practices—data stays local, nothing cloud-uploaded.
  - Consider code splitting/lazy-load for WebLLM model/code.
  - Support efficient context-passing to WebLLM (data scoping, window size, etc.).
  - Must work well on both desktop and mobile viewports.

## Bounty/Labels
- idea-credit: @TheJollyLaMa
- bounty: 4 BNUT
- V3.0.0
